Learn Python From a JavaScript Angle (15): Modules
Next up, let's talk about modules in Python. JavaScript has its own concept of modules too, so this is a nice place to compare the two.

If you've ever used tools like Webpack or Vue CLI, you've probably already got a decent feel for what a module is. And even if you haven't touched Webpack yet, you've likely run into a bit of code like
language-js shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
syntax that Node.js developers reach for all the time:
These days the more famous one is probably ESM (ES6 Modules, a.k.a. JavaScript Modules). If you're not familiar with ESM, you might want to check out an earlier post of mine,
What is ESM (ES6 Modules or JavaScript Modules)? (article in Mandarin)
— it goes into a lot more detail.
So here's my quick summary of what a module is:
, and that file usually holds a bunch of functions — or features, if you like — that we can use. The point is we can import that file and use it from other files.
For example, say we have five Python files, and all of them need to do addition and say hello:
language-py shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

# ch1.py
print(1 + 2)
print(f'Hello Ray!')

# ch2.py
print(10 + 23)
print(f'Hello Mike!')

# ch3.py
print(12 + 6)
print(f'Hello Ming!')

# ch4.py
print(105+ 9)
print(f'Hello Jack!')

# ch5.py
print(189 + 256)
print(f'Hello Charles!')

Doesn't look like a big deal, right? But picture this: what if we had 100 pages that needed this? You'd be writing the same code 100 times. Sure, you might think "eh, it's fine, I'll just copy-paste." Okay, say you've finished pasting all 100 pages. Then the next day your boss tells you to change "Hello" into Chinese — now you're editing it 100 times again. Maybe you're thinking "hah, I'll just use find-and-replace!" Wow, good for you!
Just kidding — the meme above is just a joke.
But if you make good use of modularization, you only have to edit that one module file. So let's figure out how to modularize the example code above!
Same as always, let's start with how JavaScript exports a module. Here's an ESM export:
To bring it in, you have to use
language-html shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

<script type="module">
  import app from './app.js';
  console.log(app.myName); // 'Ray'
  app.sayHello(); // Hello Ray
  console.log(app.obj.myName); // Ray
  console.log(app.arr[0]); // 0
</script>

Now let's swing the camera back to Python.

the results. Compare that with JavaScript, where you also have to use the

Nope. You just declare a dictionary as usual, and you don't even need

Now for something a bit simpler — the
in the examples above. And just plainly importing a specific file like that is actually one way of importing everything, so you can directly use all the functions and dictionaries inside
has other tricks too. Take the earlier
— every time you want to use a function from the module, you have to write
and so on. At that point
starts to feel long and annoying. So you can use
to rename what you're bringing in, and then call it by the new name:

# example.py
import module as mu

print(mu.add(100, 25)) # 125

print(mu.sayHi('Ray')) # Hello Ray!

print(mu.dic['myName']) # Ray
As I mentioned, plainly importing a module means you're pulling the whole package into your file. But sometimes you only want one specific thing from it, and that's where
For example, if I only want to import the

# example.py
from module import dic

print(dic['myName']) # Ray
That basically reads "from xxx import xxx method." Compare that with the JavaScript import:
You can see the two are written differently — one starts with
. From a JavaScript angle, the gist is "import xxx, whose source is xxx."

I'll warn you upfront: if you look up
, the output is insanely detailed and insanely long, so I'm only grabbing part of it:
language-txt shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast

Function definitions
********************

A function definition defines a user-defined function object (see
section The standard type hierarchy):

   funcdef                   ::= [decorators] "def" funcname "(" [parameter_list] ")"
               ["->" expression] ":" suite
   decorators                ::= decorator+
   decorator                 ::= "@" assignment_expression NEWLINE
   parameter_list            ::= defparameter ("," defparameter)* "," "/" ["," [parameter_list_no_posonly]]
                        | parameter_list_no_posonly
   parameter_list_no_posonly ::= defparameter ("," defparameter)* ["," [parameter_list_starargs]]
                                 | parameter_list_starargs
   parameter_list_starargs   ::= "*" [parameter] ("," defparameter)* ["," ["**" parameter [","]]]
                               | "**" parameter [","]
   parameter                 ::= identifier [":" expression]
   defparameter              ::= parameter ["=" expression]
   funcname                  ::= identifier

A function definition is an executable statement.  Its execution binds
the function name in the current local namespace to a function object
(a wrapper around the executable code for the function).  This
function object contains a reference to the current global namespace

You can get a similar effect on your own module, though the description won't be nearly as detailed:
At this point someone might ask how to get a description to show up like
does. It's really simple — just use comments:
# NAME: this line becomes the module's description
# FUNCTIONS: very simple math, but it returns an integer
# FUNCTIONS: this is a method that greets someone
# the explanatory comment has to go before def — putting it inside def won't show up
# DATA entries don't show a comment description
# writing it here doesn't help either
By now you can probably see how handy
are. Alright, that's it for today

A few days ago I got a notice from the animal protection office, basically saying my dog hasn't been spayed and I should take her in soon or face a fine. My dog is already around 7 to 8 years old, but after a professional assessment the vet still recommended spaying her. I'll admit I was against it at first, but after hearing the vet out I figured it was better to just go along with it — for the dog's health above all.

Let's talk about modules in Python. JavaScript has its own idea of modules too, so we'll compare the two side by side — how to split your code into a separate file, export it, and import it back. Plus a couple of handy built-ins, dir() and help(), that make exploring modules way easier.
